quick_facts.label: Qualification level
quick_facts.value: A postgraduate commerce degree; UGC’s master’s-degree framework requires an undergraduate degree (or a university-recognised equivalent) for entry to a master’s programme.

quick_facts.label: Typical duration
quick_facts.value: Two years is a common M.Com format: IGNOU lists a 24-month minimum duration, and the National Credit Framework describes a two-year master’s pathway after a three-year bachelor’s degree with a minimum of 80 credits.

quick_facts.label: Eligibility is university-specific
quick_facts.value: A bachelor’s degree is the baseline, but subject combinations, minimum marks, category relaxations, and recognised qualifications vary by institution. For example, the University of Delhi’s M.Com rules specify eligible commerce/business-related bachelor’s qualifications and minimum aggregate marks by category.

quick_facts.label: Study modes
quick_facts.value: M.Com is offered in campus-based, open-and-distance-learning, and online formats. IGNOU lists both ODL M.Com and online M.Com options.

quick_facts.label: Illustrative published fee
quick_facts.value: As displayed by IGNOU for its online M.Com programme when reviewed, the programme fee is ₹10,200 per year; examination fees are excluded. This is an institutional example, not a national fee benchmark.

sections.heading: Eligibility: a bachelor’s degree is the starting point, but exact rules differ
paragraphs: For formal master’s programmes, UGC’s minimum-standard regulations state that admission requires successful completion of a three-year undergraduate degree, the prescribed undergraduate credits, or an equivalent qualification recognised by the concerned university. Universities then set their own programme-specific criteria, including accepted disciplines, minimum marks, reservation-related relaxations, and documentation requirements.

The University of Delhi provides a useful illustration rather than a universal rule: its published M.Com admission bulletin accepts specified three- or four-year commerce and business-related degrees and requires applicants to appear in the designated CUET-PG paper. Its Commerce department also lists minimum aggregate eligibility of 50% for UR/OBC-NCL/EWS applicants and 45% for SC/ST/PwBD applicants, subject to programme-specific eligibility. Applicants should therefore check the target university’s admission bulletin for their admission cycle rather than assume that every M.Com accepts every bachelor’s degree on identical terms.
For flexible study, IGNOU’s listed M.Com ODL and online programmes accept graduates in any discipline, illustrating that eligibility can be broader in some institutions and modes.
bullets: Check whether the university requires B.Com/B.Com (Hons.) or also accepts business, economics, management, or other bachelor’s degrees.
Check the qualifying-mark rule and category-specific relaxation in the current admission bulletin.
For CUET-PG universities, confirm the applicable test-paper code and whether the programme uses CUET-PG for that cycle.

sections.heading: Duration and academic structure
paragraphs: A two-year M.Com remains a widely used structure. IGNOU lists a minimum duration of 24 months and a maximum completion window of 48 months for its M.Com ODL and online offerings. Under the UGC National Credit Framework, a two-year master’s programme following a three-year bachelor’s degree carries a minimum of 80 credits, normally split as at least 40 credits in each year.

The actual calendar, semester pattern, credit total, attendance requirements, exit options, and maximum completion period are determined by the university and mode of study. Distance and online programmes may allow a longer maximum period than a regular campus programme.
bullets: Common format: four semesters over two academic years.
Confirm the university’s maximum time allowed for completion, particularly for ODL/online enrolment.
Confirm whether the programme is regular, ODL, or online and whether that format is recognised for the intended career or recruitment purpose.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: M.Com curricula are designed around advanced commerce and business analysis rather than a single nationally prescribed subject list. IGNOU describes its online M.Com as providing advanced knowledge in commerce, trade, accounting, finance, taxation, and business management, alongside analytical problem-solving, managerial and entrepreneurial capability, research aptitude, and applied employability skills.

At the University of Delhi, the M.Com programme for entrants from July 2025 is structured under its Postgraduate Curriculum Framework aligned to NEP 2020. The department’s published curriculum materials include electives such as customer relationship management, entrepreneurial finance, ESG and business sustainability, financial modelling using Excel, and marketing for start-ups. Course titles, compulsory papers, electives, assessment, and project requirements should be checked in the relevant university’s current syllabus.
bullets: Typical themes include accounting, finance, taxation, business environment, trade, management, research methods, and analytical/quantitative skills.
Electives can add applied areas such as financial modelling, sustainability, entrepreneurship, marketing, or international business.
A programme’s syllabus is the authoritative source for its paper list and credit requirements.

sections.heading: Admission routes and application process
paragraphs: Admission is administered by each university. UGC’s master’s-degree regulations provide that admission is made on merit using criteria notified by the university, while taking account of applicable UGC/statutory-body norms and reservation policy. In practice, a university may use an entrance examination, CUET-PG, qualifying-degree merit, or a combination specified in its own bulletin.

For example, the University of Delhi’s 2025 two-year postgraduate bulletin states that M.Com applicants must meet programme-specific eligibility and appear in the relevant CUET paper, identified there as COQP08. This is dated institutional guidance, not a standing rule for all universities or future cycles; applicants should use the bulletin and examination notice for the cycle in which they apply.
bullets: Identify target universities and study mode before applying.
Read the current programme-specific eligibility table, not only the general postgraduate eligibility page.
If CUET-PG is required, register for the exact paper specified by the university and retain scorecard and qualification documents for counselling/registration.

sections.heading: Fees: compare programme-level charges, not just tuition
paragraphs: There is no single India-wide M.Com fee. Fees vary materially by public/private institution, location, study mode, category, scholarships, and additional charges. As a dated published example, IGNOU lists ₹10,200 per year for its online M.Com and notes that examination fees are not included; its ODL M.Com page also lists ₹10,200 per year. These figures should be verified on the admission portal before payment because institutions can revise fees and ancillary charges by session.

When comparing offers, check tuition or programme fee, registration/development charges, examination fees, study-material or technology charges where applicable, hostel and living costs for regular programmes, and any refund policy stated in the admission prospectus.
bullets: Verify whether examination fees are additional.
For ODL/online options, verify the programme fee, examination fee, and any required in-person assessment or support-centre costs.

sections.heading: Common next steps after M.Com
paragraphs: M.Com can support a research route to a Ph.D. in Commerce or a related discipline. Under UGC’s Ph.D. Regulations, higher education institutions may admit candidates who qualify for specified national fellowships/tests through interview and/or through an institutional entrance test; universities publish their own eligibility thresholds, discipline requirements, supervisor availability, and admission process.

For teaching and research eligibility, UGC-NET includes Commerce as a subject. NTA’s UGC-NET FAQ advises candidates to take NET in their postgraduate subject; it specifically addresses an M.Com holder and directs candidates to use their postgraduate subject or, where the subject is unavailable, a related subject.

M.Com holders may also pursue professional pathways. ICAI states that commerce graduates or postgraduates with at least 55% marks may register for CA Intermediate through the Direct Entry Route; candidates should confirm the current ICAI registration, study-period, and examination rules before applying. A B.Ed. can be another route for candidates seeking school-teaching preparation: NCTE’s published B.Ed. norms set eligibility at at least 50% marks in either the bachelor’s and/or master’s degree, subject to applicable reservation and institution rules.
bullets: Ph.D. in Commerce: check the institution’s current doctoral admission notice, research areas, entrance/interview process, and supervisor capacity.
UGC-NET (Commerce): use the current NTA information bulletin and select the appropriate postgraduate/related subject as permitted.
CA Intermediate: verify eligibility and registration requirements directly with ICAI; the direct-entry threshold cited is 55% for commerce graduates/postgraduates.
B.Ed.: check the current NCTE-recognised institution’s admission rules and applicable state/university requirements.
